comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of L.W. (Lauron William) de Laurence, one of the pioneers of the mail order occult business in 19th century America.  Born in Ohio in 1868, he moved to Chicago when that was the heart of the US occult scene and established a mail order company that supplied books and "spiritual" (i.e., magical) supplies to practitioners across the country. He had (and earned) a reputation as a book pirate, but his editions of The Long Lost Friend and Secrets of the Psalms played a major role in getting material from Pennsylvania Dutch folk magic into African-American magic. Late in life he was consecrated as a bishop in one of the esoteric Christian lineages, and passed on the consecration to the pastors of several African-American Spiritual churches, helping to start lineages that survive to this day. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

replies.content:
3) “Magic works in practice but not in theory.” - Peter J. Carroll.

Chaos magic seems to have began with the questions “(A) if contradictory religious belief systems can all make effective magic, what does that prove about those religions? Does a belief need to be true to be the basis for magic? (B) Would it make any difference if you replaced it with secular symbolism, fiction or arbitrary gibberish?”

After decades of experimentation the answers seem to be:
(A) No, magic involving unreal or nonsensical things still works, therefore magical success is not proof of religious truth. That is, magic has an independent force of its own and is not exclusively powered by being plugged into a true and potent religion. However,
(B) Yes, which symbols you use /does make a difference/; magic made with religious content behaves differently than magic made with fictional content, and /which/ specific religion (or fiction!) you use also makes some difference. That is, symbols are not arbitrary or interchangeable.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To (B): What symbols you use do indeed make a difference, and they are not arbitrary or interchangeable.

Linguistic theory has been wrestling with the nature of symbols for over a century now, and there are also Medieval antecedents to that discussion. The current consensus seems to be that an individual symbol, were such a thing ever to exist in isolation, might be arbitrary, but no effective symbol exists in isolation in any known culture. Rather, symbols are parts of entire symbolic systems, where the system constrains every symbol in it to such an extent that no such symbol can ever be arbitrary, nor--with trivial exceptions--can it be interchangeable with any other symbol in that system, or in any other system.

I could probably spend half a day laying out the theory (or cluster of theories) about this in considerable detail, but here is not the right place. It is, however, a very rich and productive body of theory, and it forms the basis on which I personally have come to my own understanding of how magic can be powerful and how it works.

comments.title: Geomantic Spirits
comments.content:
Greetings, Mr. Greer


I have some reservations about working with the Geomantic Spirits. Sources such as Cornelius Agrippa list them as demons, and the lore says one shouldn't interact with them without the intercession of the Celestial Intelligences which rule over them.

In your manual on Geomancy, you instruct the Geomancer to draw the sigil of the Geomantic spirit before asking a question. How much danger can the Geomancer expose himself to by working with these spirits, and can a invocation to a planetary intelligence be said before consulting a Geomantic Spirit?

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Spirits
replies.content:
I'm guessing that you meant to end that sentence with something like "...intelligences help?"

Okay, first of all, if you don't feel comfortable working with the geomantic spirits, then don't work with the geomantic spirits. Plenty of capable geomancers don't invoke them; it's been years since I've used the ritual method of preparation for a reading, and I still get good clear results.

Second, there's a difference between evoking a spirit in a full-blown magical ceremony, on the one hand, and making a much more casual contact with the same spirit's energies on the other. The method given in trad geomantic books is the latter. All you're doing is attuning yourself to the planetary energies as they are reflected in the energy body of the Earth -- that reflection, btw, is basically what the spirits in question are. People have been doing that safely for a very long time. Still, as already noted, if you don't feel comfortable with that kind of working, don't do it.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Spirits
replies.content:
Sorry, I accidentally posted the original message before it was complete.

My reticence towards the Geomantic Spirits arose from my brief acquaintance with E.A. Koetting's work. He is a dark magician who has written several modern grimoires. One focused particularly on Azazel. I experienced a lot of strange and unpleasant synchronicities concerning that spirit after just reading that grimoire, and sense it would seem one of the Geomantic Spirits is named Zazel, which is a variant of the same name as Azazel, I became cautious and a little bit fearful having experienced Spirit Obsession in the past. Needless to say, I stay far, far away from E.A. Koetting and his baneful material after my negative experience.

Your reply has inspired me to get over my fear, because as you pointed out, people have been working with the Geomantic Spirits a long time safely.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Spirits
replies.content:
It's a sensible concern. Do you practice a daily banishing ritual? Alongside its many other benefits, that's a good way to keep noxious spirits at bay.

While the names of Zazel and Azazel are related linguistically, by the way, they're not actually the same spirit. Zazel (ZAZL in Hebrew) is a spirit of Saturn, and his name has a gematria value of 45, one of the numbers of Saturn. Azazel is AaZAZL in Hebrew, with an initial Ayin, so its gematria is 115, not a Saturnine number -- rather, it has Mercurial energies.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Spirits
replies.content:
That is very interesting that Azazel is in fact a Mercurial spirit. E.A. Koetting and his followers present Azazel as a Saturnian spirit, but based on your input it would seen as though Koetting simply made the same linguistic analysis I made rather than sound insight. Knowing Azazel's true nature shines a light on Koetting's Book of Azazel, considering that Mercury is the god of trickery as well as intellect. It would seem Azazel is making sport of Koetting and those that follow his teachings.

I was for a time practicing a daily banishing ritual, but became lazy after discovering that a bowl of vinegar can act an effective ward against spirits. Though recently I found out the hard way that natural magic should be complimented with ritual for sure. Spirits don't like active wards, and definitely seek to find ways around them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Right. You've got a physical state; you have, rather closely connected to that, an etheric state (the condition of your life energy); you've got an emotional or, as occultists would say, an astral state, which is the condition of your astral body and the emotional energies that move through it; you've got a mental state, which is the condition of your mental sheath, the structure of mental plane substance you're slowly building into a mental body over many lives; and you've got a spiritual state, which (since you don't have even the first stirrings of a spiritual body yet) is the condition of the link between you and the realms of spiritual realities. Someone whose spiritual state is of a low grade can't conceive of a difference between the spiritual realms and their own emotions; at an intermediate grade, you begin to get a glimpse now and again of the spiritual realms as objective realities that don't necessarily care what you think or feel about them; at a relatively high grade, to borrow a bit of Tolkien, you live in both realms at once.

replies.content: Got it in one. As you proceed through your human lives, you develop the mental sheath into a mental body, and in the process you open up a connection with the spiritual planes further up. Once your mental body is complete you don't incarnate as a human being any more, as you've made the connection with spirit and can begin to develop a sheath on the lowest of the spiritual planes, the plane of the human spirit. replies.title: (no subject)

Perhaps you've met middle-aged or old people who literally haven't had a new thought in twenty years, and just keep on rehashing the same notions; now imagine that stretching out across ages. Death keeps that from happening, and gives you a chance to reboot. Once you've got the trick of reflective self-knowledge down, you can reboot without shutting down, so to speak, and so your next death is your last.

For my money, mortality is perhaps the very greatest gift the Cosmos has ever given living beings. Just think what it would be like to be unable to die. The burden of memory and of self-knowledge (warts and all!) would become simply unbearable after a few centuries, and would surely drive any human stark raving mad after thousands of years. I wouldn't have immortality if it were offered to me, not for any price.

If it was only you who were immortal, you would have lost all your childhood friends, all their children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren; and eventually the entire civilization and world into which you were born would cease to exist.

And it would be even worse if everyone was immortal. No matter how long you lived, there would always be generations of living people who were much older than you, and much wiser. Your great-grandparents and grandparents have to die before you and your age-mates can become the eldest living generation, reviled (or, hopefully, valued) for your knowledge and experience. Five or ten generations down the line from you, literally everyone would hate you with life-long burning hate: your very existence would deprive them of their own eventual adulthood and status as an elder among their people.

One thing I am aware of with wealthy people is that a great many of them are psychologically damaged by having been sent to boarding school very young. The youngest I know of were just four years old, which is not uncommon. I spent a few years in such a school myself and know very well what they're like. These are not places of privilege, but of abandonment, and to cope with it a child must change themselves at a fundamental level, for to show any sign of vulnerability is to put oneself in danger. When you don't get to go home at night for weeks on end, and also share a bedroom with several others, it turns you from a soft child into something altogether different. It is frightening to think of.

I count myself lucky, because I didn't adapt but instead rebelled and my leaving was not mourned by those in charge. But I recognise those damaged other ones, the ones who adapted - once you know, they are easy to spot and avoid.

I've interacted from time to time with some of those very rich people in my capacity as a member of the faculty at Brown University. Many of them seem to be just as our host described them. A few, however, take a far more active role in the world around them. They are distinguished from most people--including most other rich people--not by such things as intelligence and culture, but by ruthlessness, determination, and willingness to take any risk whatever to preserve their own wealth and power. For an earlier example, think of Cornelius Vanderbilt, who began as a sailor and ferryboat hand, but died one of the richest men in the USA. Utterly ruthless, and highly intelligent.

The famous quote from the Gilded Age, "Millionaires don't consult astrologers; billionaires do," is usually attributed to J.P. Morgan, but it actually fits what we know of Commodore Vanderbilt better.

Hi JMG,

You are probably already familiar with this. If not, this article might be of some interest to you or MM readers: https://hareesh.org/blog/2016/2/5/the-real-story-on-the-chakras

Similarly to what you do with medieval Latin, Christopher Wallis is digging through old Sanskrit texts and shares the gems he finds there with others. In this article he gives a few thoughts on how chakras, chakra systems, and related practices were described in the original Tantric texts. They look suspiciously simiral to the way the Tree of Life is approached in Cabala. It is quite possible that Tree of Life is a not-too-distant relative of one of these old chakra systems.

I also have a question this week.

W.E. Butler mentions briefly in 'The Magician, His Training and Work' that the aspirants who display any talent in the Magical Arts are put through a more rigorous training discipline than 'normal' people. Contemplating this idea I could think of two reasons for this.

First, these talented people would be more likely to stumble into stronger blowbacks earlier than the rest of the students, so training slower and more deliberately would be a basic safety precaution for the sake of everyone within the possible blast radius. Giordano Bruno looks like a victim of this trap. His Magic system was so heliocentric that even his death came through exposure to concentrated sunlight.

Second, someone for whom Magic comes too early and too easy, particularly at a young age, has all the chances to slip into a runaway ego-trip. Taking a slower route would help them to build healthy humility early on and, as a result, to go deeper and achieve more in the long run. It looks that Aleister Crowley might have been an example of the opposite in action.

Does it sound fair? Is there anything else to it?

replies.title: Re: Demon at a friends house
replies.content: Is your friend willing to learn the Sphere of Protection? The best way to chase off a noxious spirit is to perform daily banishings, and it's best for the person who lives in the place to be the one who does that.

replies.title: Re: Demon at a friends house

What you said about the demon's trying not to lose its "food"--that's surely a big part of it. They seem to feed on fear.

One of the things that may help is that many demons are actually somewhat like ordinary human bullies: they act strong and dangerous, but are actually rather weak and timid beneath all the bluster. Like human bullies, they can get most of their power by playing on the terrors and doubts of their victims, which they try to magnify in various ways.

comments.title: Rainbow thaumaturgy
comments.content:
As a gay man in a twenty-year committed relationship and raised by a lesbian mother and, I have been participating in gay events in some form or another since I was a wee child – playing with legos on the floor at the gay community center as pride events were being planned above me, singing in gay choirs, swimming with gay swim teams, marching in pride parades, attending pride parades and parties. But this year's gay pride events have more than left me cold, they have left me with a sense of foreboding.

I was in downtown while the booths and concessions for Pittsburgh's pride weekend were being set up, and I walked past a trailer with a bank of telephones, comfortable couches, and a sign asking "How much unconscious bias is in your thoughts? Listen to find out." I have no idea what gimmick they were using with the phones and recordings, nor why anyone would subject themselves to that kind of brainwashing, but it was disturbing enough that I avoided the pride events altogether.

Back home in New York City I saw endless rainbow flags and odd photos from past pride events flashing up on the city's public information video kiosks. Cacomagic, indeed, but it didn't have the desired effect on me at all. Again, I avoided the pride events altogether.

I texted my partner, "Rainbow flags are everywhere in New York – the gay mafia has taken over. Restaurants and salons are afraid they will lose their customers if they don't fly the colors. Bodes very ill for the next swing back of the pendulum. This must be similar to how Germans felt when not putting up swastikas could get your shop boycotted. We are frighteningly close to a majority cheering a fascist bully into office. As always, that particular flavor of lunacy comes from overreach by the left. Strange times!" That is a text written by a liberal queer to his liberal boyfriend.

Stephen texted back from Paris, "It has been so much more restrained here. I didn't even know it was the Big Queer Weekend until I saw some kids in gender-bender clothes on the metro and realized that there are a lot of fit young men wandering around together. I had thought they were here for the Women's World Cup."

So my question is how can you protect yourself and your larger community when the cacomagicians take over? The corporate tie-ins, self-appointed thought police, and competition to be the most gay friendly city in the US are dangerous. Who had the cojones to put up rainbow flags when that could lose you customers or get your windows broken? What is the point to these phatic emblems, now sanitized of all risk, other than to draw in greater profits? When that profit engine stops delivering, who will be blamed? When the magic symbol of the rainbow can't answer prayers, what curses will be heaped upon it?

Is there a way to insulate oneself from the exploitative energy of narrative hijackers? Is there a way to protect the gay community from the blowback that will follow from letting parasites feed off a too-successful social movement? Or is distancing oneself from a corrupted movement the best protection?

–Christophe

replies.title: Re: Rainbow thaumaturgy
replies.content:
Dear Christophe,

There is another issue as well, the new pride flag, "more colors more pride," as can be seen here: https://morecolormorepride.com/

The issue is that having a black stripe brings in evil energies. Imagine if a rainbow with a black and brown stripe should actually disfigure the skies for a moment. Children would start crying and adults would cower in terror.

And so with some sadness, I've decided to in every way to distance myself from the community that I have participated in for my entire young adulthood. I think it has morphed into something evil and is horribly corrupted. Now I do not define myself regarding my sexuality and avoid people whose auras scream "QUEER".

replies.title: Re: Rainbow thaumaturgy
replies.content:
I wish I had a simple answer to that extremely complex and many-layered question. I know a lot of gay men are distancing themselves these days from the LBTQ+ end of things as that has been turned into an instrument of corporate virtue signaling and political manipulation; it occurs to me that if you know other gay men who feel the same way as you do, it might be time to create your own spaces, the sort of thing gay men had before all the other letters in the alphabet crashed the party. (I understand some lesbians are beginning to do the same thing in response to parallel changes on their end of the community -- and sensibly so.)

I would certainly encourage you to consider dropping the rainbow symbolism. The gay male community had its own symbols back in the day; it might be worth doing a bit of research, or talking to some guys who were part of the scene back in the day, to identify those symbols, reclaim them, and begin to craft a group identity separate from a movement that has lost touch with its purpose and become just another part of the machinery.

replies.title: gay symbols
replies.content:
On a tour bus in England I saw a man with lovely pansies tattooed on his forearms. Oscar Wilde wore green carnations, lesbians sometimes used violets as a signal. The pink triangle forced on homosexual prisoners by the Nazis was rather successfully used by ACT-UP and other anti-AIDS activists as a symbol. We have reached a strange point when Mayor Pete and his husband are considered too normal, by some, to even count as gay. Some of this pandering by corporate America reminds me of my reaction to the advent of Jockey for Women--"We wanted their power and they gave us their underwear."

I'm going to jump in with a wild surmise. I wonder if so many people seek an identity in their sexuality or their fandom or other aspects of their personality because there is so little to identify proudly with in one's livelihood these days. Professions can have professional pride: nurse, doctors, teachers --and some skilled trades one can imagine having it--truckers and farmers, construction, come to mind, but key data operator, retail associate, call center operator, administrative assistant?

comments.title: The Spirit and the Sword
comments.content: Good Morning! I got The Spirit and the Sword for my birthday a few days ago, and in looking through it I realized [as I had suspected] the sword I own is not suitable for practice, being too long and too heavy [it's a broadsword]. I can barely draw the thing, as I'm on the short side. I was wondering if you had any advice as to how to get a proper 'fitting' sword. If you covered it in the book I haven't reached that part. replies.title: Re: The Spirit and the Sword
replies.content: Have you tried the cheap wooden ones in various lengths?  Once you find THE length there are companies that will make you one (not cheap).

replies.title: Re: The Spirit and the Sword
replies.content:
A walking stick is a good thing to use. The problem is that nearly all replica swords -- including the ones that talk about how realistic they are -- are much heavier and clumsier than real swords. The real swords I've handled -- including a Civil War infantry sword of exactly the kind The Spirit and the Sword is intended for -- are literally half the weight of good replicas, and a small fraction of the weight of bad ones.

Hold your arm straight out to your side, and get a friend with a tape measure. Your sword should be no longer than the distance between the center of your chest and your fingertips, and may well be less. It should weigh no more than 3 pounds -- less if you're physically small.

replies.title: Re: The Spirit and the Sword
replies.content: They're lighter than a lot of of the SLOs ("sword-like objects") being marketed these days, but they're still too heavy. I have a replica Civil War Union noncommissioned officer's sword from Museum Replicas, and have handled an actual sword of exactly the same type. The actual sword was two-thirds the weight, if that, and the difference in responsiveness in the hand had to be felt to be believed.

comments.title: Confidence
comments.content:
Last week in a response to someone else you recommended journaling to discover where their lack of confidence comes from, and then said: "When you're done with that and have a good idea of where your lack of confidence is coming from, post again on a Magic Monday and I'll teach you the next step."

I'm not the OP, but I have also found (and you've told me as such) that my major issue is lack of confidence. From doing the Dolmen Arch (and specifically one of the completion exercises) I have a good grasp on the origins:

As a child I was "special." Two things were constantly implicit in the behavior of adults around me: I was smarter, more mature, more "gifted" than my peers; and I was broken and would never succeed the way that my peers were expected to. This narrative permeated everything in my life, and I learned it the way I learned English or how to be American. Because this narrative was assumed axiomatically by the adults in my life, any deviation from it on my part was "corrected," the way any deviations in my language or culture would have been.

Thus I was expected (and came to expect myself) to over-promise and under-deliver. I was expected to demonstrate very high "potential" and to never fulfill that potential.

In brief, I was told that I "had so much potential," which was just another way of saying I wasn't good enough. Thus my lack of confidence.

So, what's the next step?

Okay, good. Now you craft an affirmation that counteracts the core of that harmful talk. Take your time figuring out what to use, and remember the basic rules of effective affirmations:

1) It should be a single declarative sentence in the present tense.

2) It should state the thing that you want as though you already have it.

3) It is phrased entirely in positive terms -- the unconscious cannot hear negatives (words like "no" and "not"). If you say to yourself "I have no fear" your unconscious will hear it as "I have fear."

Once you've chosen your affirmation, you repeat it to yourself many times a day. You're using positive self-talk to overcome the effects of negative self-talk and other-talk. It doesn't matter if you don't believe it; keep repeating it, and the deep levels of your mind will come to believe it.

One I used in a similar situation -- I was never allowed to feel that I was any good at anything -- was "I am the master." It worked extremely well.

comments.title: Kundalini and Dragon Currents

Manly P. Hall's discussion of kundalini in Man, Grand Symbol of the Mysteries draws parallels between several Eastern and Western symbols, and Ida and Pingala (the white and red nadis which form a helix ascending the spinal column) are easy analogs to the white and red ascending dragons. Also, I remember reading in either Fortune and Knight's The Circuit of Force or in Butler's How to Read the Aura that Eastern traditions tend to associate the currents with the midline of the body, while Western traditions tend to associate them with the limbs (the Rising Dragons exercise from The Druid Magic Handbook and the Dolmen Arch are obvious examples of the Western limb association). These, along with the difference of Eastern traditions typically working with the etheric realm while Western traditions work with the astral realm, makes me wonder.

Relatedly, the caduceus seems like a straightforward depiction of the process of kundalini awakening and also of the process of the dragons awakening under Vortigern's tower. The lightning flash and the serpent from Cabala seem related as well. Obviously all of this is prime fodder for meditation, but from a research standpoint I'm wondering about:

2) Additional examples of the process being discussed/depicted in spiritual systems/mythology,

and 3) a possible (generic) name for the process/phenomenon behind all of these depictions.

replies.title: Re: Kundalini and Dragon Currents

3) We don't know what's behind it all, so slapping a label on it may be premature.
